response: interfaces offer flexibility that abstract classes cannot match because they allow unrelated classes to share behavior without forcing a rigid inheritance hierarchy. the main reason architects prefer them is the lack of coupling they provide. if you extend an abstract class you are locked into a single path of inheritance which limits how you can compose your code. by programming to an interface you can swap implementations easily whether they come from a local file or a remote service. this approach is like adding ingredients to a salad where each item stands alone yet contributes to the whole. you do not need to know every relationship upfront because the interface defines the contract not the implementation details.
